species: Full Stonefly + Caddisfly Hatch -- mass emergence wing-buzz, spring river, April
scientific_name: Plecoptera (Pteronarcys californica + multi-species) + Trichoptera (Rhyacophila, Hydropsyche, Brachycentrus + multi-species)
sound_type: Mass emergence wing-buzz. Stonefly (Plecoptera): wing-beat 30-40 Hz (giant salmonfly Pteronarcys californica), 60-120 Hz harmonics; individually audible at close range. Mid-sized stonefly species: 60-80 Hz wing-beat. Caddisfly (Trichoptera): wing-beat 200-400 Hz, audible only as combined mass. Combined joint hatch: 30-400 Hz full lower-to-mid spectrum. Combined amplitude at river's edge during peak emergence: 75-90 dB. Duration: mid-morning through late afternoon during active mating flight. River corridor scale: miles of simultaneous emergence along temperature gradient. THE FIRST INSECT SOUND IN THE SPS ENGINE.
ecosystem: Spring river, salmonid-quality Pacific Northwest stream systems. Cold, clean, well-oxygenated water with stable gravel streambed. Water temperature 10-12°C emergence trigger (late April lower-elevation streams). Key rivers: Methow River (Okanogan County WA), Yakima River, North Fork Stillaguamish, Hoh River (Olympic NP). Emergence front moves upstream with warming temperatures (lower elevations first, higher elevations weeks later).
season: April (peak emergence Pacific Northwest lower-elevation rivers). Water temperature triggered at 10-12°C. Duration: hours per day, days per hatch event.
family: Multi-species. Order Plecoptera (stoneflies): ~3,500 species worldwide, ~600 North America. Order Trichoptera (caddisflies): ~14,500 species worldwide. Joint emergence when water temperature window coincides.
milestone: FIRST INSECT SOUND IN THE SPS ENGINE. Degree 329 opens the engine's entomological acoustic taxonomy after 328 degrees of mammals, birds, fish, amphibians, and abiotic events. Class Insecta enters at E=9 -- mass biological action, maximum acoustic impact. Giant salmonfly (Pteronarcys californica) as primary stonefly indicator. Stoneflies: most sensitive water quality indicator in North American streams. Mass simultaneous emergence as antipredator swamping strategy. Caddisfly swarms visible on weather radar over productive rivers (Yakima, Kootenai). Fish rise response: surface-feeding fish add acoustic layer (splash) to emergence event. Bat predation post-dusk: bat echolocation adds high-frequency component to post-emergence river corridor.

coordinates.description: Representative: Methow River, Okanogan County, WA. 48.35°N 120.18°W. Major annual salmonfly hatch late April. Also: Yakima River (46.6°N 120.5°W), North Fork Stillaguamish, Hoh River (47.86°N 123.93°W within Olympic NP).

acoustic_data.stonefly_fundamental: Giant salmonfly (Pteronarcys californica) wing-beat: 30-40 Hz. Individual: 40-55 dB at 1m. Mass density (hundreds to thousands per linear meter): 70-85 dB across corridor.
acoustic_data.stonefly_harmonics: 60-120 Hz from wing-beat harmonics and medium-sized stonefly species (60-80 Hz wing-beat).
acoustic_data.caddisfly_fundamental: Wing-beat 200-400 Hz (species-size dependent). Audible only as combined mass -- no individual caddisfly audible at normal listening distance. Millions of individuals: hiss/roar filling high-mid acoustic range.
acoustic_data.combined_profile: 30-400 Hz continuous coverage. 75-90 dB at river's edge during peak. Duration: mid-morning to late afternoon (active mating flight). Character: low roar (stonefly component) + high roar/hiss (caddisfly component) + river background + fish splash (rise).
acoustic_data.e9_calibration: E=9 through mass biological action. Individual insects produce minimal sound; thousands simultaneously create acoustic environment overwhelming the river's baseline. The mass emergence mechanism is distinct from all other E=9 sources in the engine (geological, meteorological, single-organism). First insect E=9: new calibration category.

nodes.title: FIRST INSECT SOUND IN SPS ENGINE -- Class Insecta at E=9
nodes.description: 328 degrees of non-insect sounds (mammals, birds, fish, amphibians, abiotic). Degree 329 opens the entomological taxonomy at maximum impact: mass emergence, thousands simultaneous, E=9. The most spectacular Pacific Northwest insect acoustic event as the engine's insect debut.

nodes.title: Stoneflies as Water Quality Indicators -- The River Health Certificate
nodes.description: Order Plecoptera (especially large species like Pteronarcys californica) is the most sensitive aquatic insect to water degradation. Mass stonefly emergence certifies excellent stream quality: cold, clean, oxygenated, stable substrate. The acoustic event is simultaneously a health assessment.

nodes.title: Synchronized Mass Emergence as Antipredator Swamping Strategy
nodes.description: Individual emergence: nearly silent, high predation risk. Mass simultaneous emergence: overwhelms predator field. The acoustic consequence is proportionate: thousands simultaneously fill the river corridor with sound. The evolution of mass emergence created the acoustic event.

nodes.title: Joint Hatch Frequency Architecture: 30-400 Hz
nodes.description: Stonefly wing-beat 30-40 Hz (giant salmonfly) to 60-120 Hz (harmonics + medium species). Caddisfly wing-beat 200-400 Hz. Combined: continuous coverage from 30 to 400 Hz. Low roar (stonefly) + high roar/hiss (caddisfly) = full lower-to-mid acoustic spectrum from biological source.

cross_references.relationship: E=9 + E=9 = E=18. THE HIGHEST COMBINED ENERGY PAIRING IN THE ENGINE. Crossover thrash mass human aggression (The Accused, organized simultaneous velocity) paired with mass biological emergence (stonefly + caddisfly, organized simultaneous flight initiation). Both events defined by mass simultaneous action.

Multi-species biological sound at E=7. Joint hatch at E=9. Two different insect/biological mass events. The chorus (three species, three layers) at E=7; the joint hatch (two orders, millions of individuals) at E=9. Mass action calibrated by number of individuals, not number of species.

The Hoh River ecosystem at maximum storm (E=8) vs. the same ecosystem type at maximum spring emergence (E=9). The biological event surpasses the meteorological event in energy at the river corridor scale.
